report: The patient initially lies still and exhibits a blank stare, then begins to move their arms and head, showing signs of restlessness. This is followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, along with right arm flexion and left arm extension. Tonic stiffening and clonic jerks develop, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks. The patient remains unresponsive at the end.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a rightward head version and either right arm extension or flexion. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension (with some observations noting right arm flexion). This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, often accompanied by rhythmic jerks.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a leftward head version and left arm flexion, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. There is also a slight rightward head version with subtle right arm extension and rhythmic clonic jerks that spread bilaterally.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. Subsequently, there is a progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight head version to the left. There is a subtle right arm flexion, which progresses to involve both arms in a tonic–clonic manner.

The patient initially sits upright with arms crossed, then leans back and lies down. There is a brief period of restlessness before she settles into a relaxed position, showing no signs of seizure activity.

The patient initially maintains a blank stare, followed by a slight rightward head version. They then exhibit right arm extension with the left arm remaining flexed, indicating fencer posturing, or display unilateral arm flexion, both of which transition into bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight head turn to the left and right. There is a subtle right arm flexion, transitioning into a more pronounced leftward head version. The patient then shows bilateral tonic-clonic movements, characterized by rhythmic jerks and loss of consciousness.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle head movements and slight arm flexion. Gradual progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity is observed, characterized by rhythmic jerking motions.
The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, which then progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight head turn to the left and right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.
Blank stare, followed by subtle head movements and slight arm flexion, progressing to rightward head version with right arm extension, then evolving into bilateral tonic-clonic activity.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, subsequently progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version or head turning to the right and right arm extension. In some instances, head turning to the left and right with arm flexion is observed. This is subsequently followed by progression or spread to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. A single observation notes a subtle right arm extension preceding the bilateral tonic-clonic activity, with slight head movement to the right and a blank stare.

The patient consistently exhibits an initial blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. This is subsequently followed by progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerking of both limbs. In one instance, the patient was observed sitting upright initially, then leaning back with left leg flexion accompanying the right arm extension before the onset of generalized convulsive activity.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by variable head movements—including rightward version and bilateral turning—and corresponding arm movements such as extension, flexion, or subtle motions. These initial signs progress to bilateral tonic-clonic movements characterized by rhythmic jerking and loss of consciousness or responsiveness.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by variable head version—either leftward or rightward—and subtle to pronounced arm movements involving the right arm, manifesting as flexion or extension. These focal features subsequently progress to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerking, indicating spread of epileptic activity to generalized convulsive seizures.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight rightward head version. They then display right arm extension while maintaining left arm flexion, indicating a fencer posture. This evolves into bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient exhibits a blank stare and slight rightward head version. There is right arm extension while the left arm remains relaxed. Gradual progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements is observed.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare followed by rightward head version with right arm extension, which progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.
The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.
The patient exhibits a blank stare and then rightward head version with right arm extension. Later, bilateral tonic-clonic movements are observed.
The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, and subsequently develops bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, while the left arm remains relatively still or relaxed. This is consistently observed across episodes, with gradual progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. Some descriptions note the patient was holding a remote during the initial phase. The sequence begins with a blank stare, then slight or pronounced rightward head version, and right arm extension, leading to generalized bilateral tonic-clonic activity.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. Subsequently, there are rhythmic clonic jerks involving both arms. Additional features include lip smacking.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and either right arm extension or flexion. This is subsequently followed by progression or spread to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. In one instance, the patient exhibits a blank stare and places their hand on their chest, without significant movement or tonic-clonic activity.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle head movements and slight arm flexion. There is rightward head version with right arm extension noted in one episode, and no visible head version or arm flexion/extension in others. Drooling is present, and a remote control is observed on the chest across multiple observations. Food spillage and residue are noted on the chest and bib in some instances. The patient remains relatively still during certain periods, while in others, there is a progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity with rhythmic jerking throughout the body. No significant unilateral posturing or tonic-clonic activity is observed in all segments.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, often accompanied by lip smacking, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. This sequence progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks, indicating a spread of seizure activity.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight head movement, often to the right, and corresponding right arm extension or flexion. This is subsequently followed by progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient exhibits an initial blank stare, followed by subtle head movements and asymmetric focal motor signs including unilateral head version (leftward or rightward) and mixed arm posturing—such as flexion of one arm with extension of the other, or isolated left arm flexion or right arm extension. These focal features progress gradually to bilateral tonic-clonic activity characterized by rhythmic jerks that spread to generalized convulsive movements.

The patient is lying still with a blank stare, followed by yawning and stretching. This progresses to a sudden leftward head version with left arm flexion and right arm extension, or bilateral head turning with arm flexion, transitioning into bilateral tonic-clonic movements characterized by rhythmic clonic jerks that spread throughout the body. There is no visible movement or signs of seizure activity initially.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by variable head and arm movements including rightward head version with right arm extension, head turning to the left and right with arm flexion, or isolated arm flexion. In some instances, lip smacking is also observed. These initial signs progress consistently to bilateral tonic-clonic movements characterized by visible clonic jerks. In one observation, the patient leans forward, extends their right arm downward while holding a laptop, places the laptop on the floor, and continues leaning forward with head down.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle head movements and slight arm flexions. In some instances, this includes rightward head version and right arm extension. The episode gradually progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic activity, characterized by rhythmic jerks and loss of consciousness.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head version—predominantly to the right—and right arm extension with concurrent left arm flexion. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements characterized by rhythmic jerks throughout the body and accompanied by loss of consciousness.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by variable motor manifestations. In some episodes, there is a subtle rightward head version accompanied by right arm extension or flexion, which progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. In other instances, the patient covers their face with both hands without any visible head version, arm flexion/extension, automatisms, or progression to tonic–clonic activity, remaining seated throughout.

The patient initially maintains a blank stare, followed by a rightward head version and right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks involving both arms and legs. Additional observations include right arm flexion with left arm extension and unresponsiveness at the end. There is no visible movement or change in posture noted at the beginning of the video.
The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, with subsequent progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by variable head movements—including turning to the left and right or rightward version—and corresponding arm motor activity, such as flexion or extension, predominantly involving the right arm. These focal features subsequently progress to bilateral tonic-clonic movements characterized by rhythmic jerks and loss of consciousness.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head turning—most commonly to the right, though bilateral movement is also observed—and unilateral arm involvement, typically manifesting as right arm extension or flexion. This focal onset progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, with earlier subtle motor signs becoming generalized in all episodes.

The patient initially holds a cup, then places it on the tray and clasps their hands together, appearing to be in a state of contemplation or prayer. A person approaches and interacts with the patient, who remains still with clasped hands throughout the interaction. In several instances, the patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rightward head version with right arm extension, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. One episode includes rhythmic jerks following the tonic-clonic activity. Another observation notes a blank stare followed by hand clasp without subsequent movement or tonic-clonic activity. Additionally, a separate event involves a blank stare followed by subtle head movements and slight arm flexion, with gradual progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity.
The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle head movements and slight arm flexion, with gradual progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity characterized by rhythmic jerking motions.
The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by head version and unilateral arm extension—either to the right or left—and progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head version (to the left or right) accompanied by arm movement—either flexion or extension, most commonly right arm extension. This evolves into bilateral tonic-clonic movements, with rhythmic jerks observed during the clonic phase.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension in a fencer posture or rightward head version with right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks. The patient remains unresponsive at the end.

The patient lies still with a neutral expression or blank stare, with no visible movement or change in posture or expression throughout the video in several observations. In other instances, the patient initially exhibits a blank stare followed by subtle head movements, including slight rightward head version. This is accompanied in some cases by right arm extension or slight arm flexion. These initial signs progress in multiple segments to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, characterized by rhythmic jerking motions. Lip smacking is noted in one instance alongside the blank stare and motor progression. There are periods with no significant movement or tonic-clonic activity, while in others, the clinical evolution includes a transition from staring and focal motor signs (rightward head version, unilateral arm extension) to generalized bilateral tonic-clonic activity.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight rightward head version and subtle or noticeable right arm extension or flexion, with the left arm remaining relaxed in some instances. The seizure activity consistently progresses from this focal onset to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, characterized by rhythmic or clonic jerks involving both arms and the entire body. In one variation, the head turning is described as shifting slightly to the right and then to the left, but the core pattern remains the same: onset with blank stare, rightward head deviation, and right arm involvement preceding generalized bilateral tonic-clonic convulsions.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head version and focal motor signs that vary in laterality: leftward head version with left arm flexion or rightward head version with right arm extension. These focal features progress to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, during which the patient becomes unresponsive.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a rightward head version and right arm extension. In one instance, the initial movement involves a leftward head turn with right arm flexion, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, with the patient turning onto the left side and then back to the right. In all other instances, the episode progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks following the rightward head version and right arm extension.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a head version to the right and extension of the right arm. This is succeeded by progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks and loss of consciousness. In some instances, head turning occurs bilaterally with arm flexion before evolving into generalized convulsive activity. The seizure semiology consistently begins with oculomotor and unilateral upper extremity involvement, spreading to bilateral motor manifestations.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, which then progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle head movements and slight arm adjustments. There is a gradual progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity, characterized by rhythmic jerking motions. In some instances, the patient shows only a blank stare with minimal head movement and no significant arm flexion or extension, remaining relatively still throughout. Occasionally, a slight head turn to the left and right is observed, along with subtle right arm flexion prior to the onset of bilateral tonic-clonic movements.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, which progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version (or head turning to the right) and right arm extension. This is subsequently followed by progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity, characterized by rhythmic jerks.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight head turn or version to the right (in most instances; one noted leftward) and subtle right arm extension. This is consistently followed by progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements characterized by rhythmic clonic jerks involving both arms. Additional features observed include lip smacking and slight forward leaning, with one instance of the patient standing up while holding a spoon with food, continuing to move slightly while maintaining a blank expression. In one segment, sudden withdrawal and movement toward the left side is noted, suggesting possible asymmetric seizure spread. The core sequence across all observations involves a staring spell, rightward head version, focal motor onset in the right arm, and secondary generalization to bilateral tonic-clonic activity.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle rightward head version and right arm extension. Gradually, there is progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, characterized by rhythmic jerking motions.

Blank stare, followed by rightward head version with initial right arm flexion or extension and left arm flexion. This progresses to involvement of both arms with rhythmic clonic jerks, evolving into bilateral tonic–clonic movements.

Blank stare followed by variable initial lateralizing features: leftward head version with left arm flexion or rightward head version with right arm extension. These focal onset motor signs progress to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic clonic jerks, which spread bilaterally and become generalized.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a rightward head version and right arm movement, which may present as either flexion or extension. This evolves into bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient exhibits a blank stare at the onset, often accompanied by lip smacking and subtle or overt rightward head version with right arm extension or flexion. In some instances, the initial phase includes only subtle head movements and unilateral arm involvement. This early focal phase consistently progresses to bilateral motor manifestations, which include tonic-clonic movements and rhythmic clonic jerks involving both arms. The evolution to bilateral tonic-clonic activity is a common feature across events, with rhythmic jerking frequently observed during the progression. Not all segments show seizure activity; in two instances, the patient is observed seated and engaged—either holding a phone to the ear or using a device near the mouth—without any signs of staring, automatisms, head deviation, unilateral movements, tonic stiffening, or clonic jerking. These non-ictal periods show no change in posture or movement throughout the observation.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare and lip smacking, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. Subsequently, there is a progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight head tilt or version to the right in most observations, with one instance of leftward head tilt. There is consistent right arm extension and occasional subtle flexion of the left arm, indicating unilateral onset. In one observation, right arm flexion is noted instead. The seizure progresses from this focal onset to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, characterized by rhythmic jerking and involving both arms. This evolution from initial lateralized signs to generalized motor activity is consistently reported across multiple segments.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight rightward head version and either right arm flexion or extension. In some instances, the right arm movement is subtle and may involve flexion or noticeable extension, while the left arm remains relatively still initially. This focal onset progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements involving both arms and the rest of the body, with rhythmic and noticeable clonic jerks observed during the generalized phase. The evolution typically begins unilaterally on the right side before spreading to become bilateral.

The patient consistently exhibits a blank stare at the onset of each episode, followed by lateralizing features that include head version and asymmetric motor activity. In multiple observations, the head turns to the right (rightward head version), accompanied by extension of the right arm, while the left arm remains relaxed or uninvolved initially. Some episodes feature a slight head version to the left or bilateral head turning with arm flexion, though the majority emphasize rightward head deviation and right arm extension. These focal features subsequently spread to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, characterized by rhythmic jerking and generalized motor involvement. The progression from initial staring to unilateral motor signs and then to bilateral convulsive activity is consistent across all episodes.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension in several episodes, with subsequent spread to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. In other instances, the patient initially shows a blank stare followed by slight leftward head version or tilt, sometimes accompanied by subtle right arm extension, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerking. Additional observations include head version to both left and right with arm flexion or combined flexion and extension, also evolving into bilateral tonic-clonic activity. Overall, the seizure semiology begins with a blank stare and asymmetric motor features that consistently generalize to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight rightward head version and right arm extension, while the left arm remains relaxed. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, with noticeable clonic jerks.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, accompanied by slight head movements that may include turns to the left and right. A subtle right arm extension is observed in some instances, along with minimal or no significant arm flexion or extension in others. In certain observations, these initial signs progress to bilateral tonic-clonic activity, characterized by rhythmic jerking movements. However, in other instances, the patient remains relatively still throughout, without notable progression of motor activity.

The patient lies on the bed with a blank stare, followed by a rightward head version and right arm extension. A leftward head version with right arm extension is also observed. Rhythmic clonic jerks occur, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, often accompanied by lip smacking, followed by head version—most commonly to the right, occasionally to the left or bilaterally—and corresponding arm movements. Rightward head version is consistently associated with right arm extension, while leftward head version may be accompanied by right arm flexion and left arm extension, resembling fencer posturing. Bilateral head version is associated with arm flexion or mixed flexion and extension. The initial focal motor signs progress to rhythmic clonic jerks, which subsequently spread to bilateral tonic-clonic activity. Some episodes begin with a subtle head tilt to the right and isolated right arm extension, with the contralateral arm remaining relaxed or flexed, before evolving into generalized bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight rightward head version (or head tilt/turn to the right) and subtle right arm extension. In most instances, this evolves into bilateral tonic-clonic movements. One observation notes head turning to the left with right arm flexion prior to bilateral tonic-clonic activity. Rhythmic clonic jerks involving both limbs are noted in one segment. In two instances, no further motor activity beyond the initial signs—such as tonic-clonic movements, significant arm movement, or visible limb jerking—is observed, with the patient remaining relatively still after the blank stare.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight rightward head version. There is a subtle right arm extension, which progresses to involve both arms in a tonic-clonic manner.

The patient consistently exhibits an initial blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, with some episodes also including a slight head turn to the left or bilateral tonic-clonic movements. The seizures uniformly progress to bilateral tonic-clonic activity, often with rhythmic jerking observed throughout the episode. Two instances specifically note identical sequences of blank stare, rightward head version, right arm extension, and subsequent bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by a rightward head version—ranging from slight to more pronounced—and right arm extension. This is subsequently followed by progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, characterized by rhythmic jerks and spreading motor activity.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle head movements or rightward head version. There is initial slight arm flexion or subtle right arm extension, which progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic activity characterized by rhythmic jerking motions and generalized motor involvement.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a rightward head version and right arm extension in multiple observations. In some instances, the head turning is bilateral (to the left and right) and may be accompanied by arm flexion or generalized arm movements. This altered state consistently progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, often with rhythmic jerks. The patient becomes unresponsive during the episode in at least one observation.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by head version to the left or right and corresponding unilateral arm movement—either flexion or extension. This is followed by progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity characterized by rhythmic jerking and loss of consciousness.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight head turn to the right and right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a rightward head version and right arm extension, which may present subtly or with slight flexion. In some instances, the episode includes hand movements near the face and subsequent clasping of the hands during a return to a blank stare. Typically, the event progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient exhibits a blank stare and slight head movement. There is subtle right arm flexion in one observation, with no significant arm flexion or extension noted in another; minimal arm flexion is also observed in a third instance. Subtle rightward head version occurs in one episode, followed by a return to neutral position. Bilateral tonic-clonic activity is observed in one segment, while the patient remains relatively still throughout another.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle rightward head version and slight right arm flexion. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head version and hand movements, particularly near the face, which then progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic activity.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight rightward head version and right arm extension, while the left arm remains relaxed. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, characterized by rhythmic jerking of both limbs.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by variable head version (rightward in most instances, leftward in one) and asymmetric arm involvement, including right arm extension or flexion and, in one case, left arm extension resembling a fencer's posture. In the majority of observations, these initial features progress to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks. One instance describes isolated blank stare with rhythmic hand movements without head version, arm flexion, or evolution into tonic-clonic activity; all other segments describe progression to bilateral tonic-clonic seizures following the initial focal features.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and subtle right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, during which the patient raises both hands to their head and then lowers them.
The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.
The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by slight rightward head version and subtle right arm extension, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.
The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, with subsequent progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle rightward head version and slight right arm flexion, which progresses to right arm extension and rhythmic clonic jerks spreading to both sides, culminating in bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by slight head movements, often with a rightward version. Subtle right arm flexion or extension is observed, sometimes accompanied by minimal right arm movement or the patient raising their right hand to the forehead. In most instances, these features progress to bilateral tonic stiffening and clonic jerks, culminating in bilateral tonic-clonic activity. One observation notes isolated rightward head version and right arm extension without progression to significant tonic-clonic movements or automatisms.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle head movements that may include rightward or bilateral shifting. There are slight arm movements, including flexion or extension in the right or left arm, with subtle right arm extension and rightward head version noted in some instances. These initial signs progress to bilateral tonic-clonic activity, characterized by gradual tonic stiffening evolving into generalized clonic jerks. The patient becomes increasingly unresponsive during the episode and remains relatively still at baseline, with no significant arm movement observed prior to seizure onset.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle rightward head version and slight right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. In one instance, the patient exhibits a blank stare followed by covering their face with both hands, without any visible arm flexion or extension, tonic stiffening, or clonic jerks. In another instance, after the blank stare and face covering, the patient shows rightward head version and right arm extension, which subsequently spreads to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by subtle rightward head version and subtle right arm extension, which progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. In some instances, the right arm extension is followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving both arms.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. Rhythmic clonic jerks are observed, spreading to involve both sides of the body in a generalized tonic-clonic pattern.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, with some descriptions noting head turning or asymmetric arm movements. This is consistently followed by progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity characterized by rhythmic and noticeable clonic jerks involving both upper and lower body regions.
The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle head movements and slight arm flexions. Gradual progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity is observed.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head versioning or turning—predominantly to the right, though bilateral turning is also observed—and corresponding arm movements including right arm extension and bilateral arm flexion. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, characterized by rhythmic jerks, unresponsiveness, loss of consciousness, and generalization to both sides of the body.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by lateralizing signs including left arm flexion with right arm extension and/or rightward head version. In some instances, head turning to the left and right accompanies arm flexion. These initial focal features progress consistently to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerking.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks, during which the patient becomes unresponsive.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head version—predominantly to the right—and corresponding arm movements, including right arm extension or bilateral arm flexion and extension. This is consistently followed by progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, often accompanied by rhythmic jerks.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head turning—variably to the left and right—with corresponding arm movements, including right arm extension in multiple observations. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic activity characterized by rhythmic jerks and loss of consciousness.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head movement to the right with right arm extension or head turning to the left with right arm flexion. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic activity characterized by rhythmic jerks.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head version (either leftward or rightward) and right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks and loss of consciousness, during which the patient becomes unresponsive.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head versioning to the right and right arm extension. In some instances, head turning to the left and right with associated arm movements is also observed. This initial phase progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, characterized by rhythmic jerking in multiple observations.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. In another instance, the patient displays a blank stare, then leans back with legs up, showing tonic stiffening and rhythmic clonic jerks involving the entire body, before returning to a seated position with unresponsiveness.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by lateral head version (either leftward or rightward) and corresponding right arm motor activity—flexion with leftward version or extension with rightward version. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements characterized by rhythmic jerks and subsequent unresponsiveness.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a rightward head version and right arm extension in the majority of episodes; one episode involved a leftward head version with right arm extension. All episodes progress to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, with rhythmic jerks observed in most instances.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head turning—predominantly to the right—with corresponding right arm extension. This focal onset progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic activity characterized by rhythmic jerks and loss of consciousness.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks, and the patient becomes unresponsive at the end.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head versioning—either to the right or left—and corresponding arm movements, including right arm extension or flexion. This is succeeded by progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity, characterized by rhythmic jerks, clonic movements, and loss of responsiveness.
The patient initially shows a blank stare, followed by head movement and arm flexion. There is a progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity with rhythmic jerking and spread of movements.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head movement and arm flexion, with progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity and rhythmic jerks. Concurrently, the patient appears to be adjusting their position, pulling the blanket over themselves tightly around their body, showing signs of discomfort, but remains still without significant movement or shaking.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head movement—including a slight turn to the left and right—and asymmetric arm movements, with subtle flexion of the left arm and extension of the right arm. This is followed by transition into bilateral tonic-clonic activity.

The patient initially holds a green folder, then removes it and adjusts the blanket. They exhibit a blank stare, followed by a slight head version to the left. The patient lifts their legs, places their hands on their head, spreads their legs apart, and then crosses them back, remaining in a reclined position throughout. There is a progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with noticeable jerking of the arms and legs.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head movement and arm flexion. There is a progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity with rhythmic jerks and uncontrolled movements.

The patient initially displays a blank stare, followed by head turning to the left and right with arm movements. The patient consistently exhibits an initial blank stare, followed by lateral head version—most commonly to the right, occasionally to the left—and concurrent focal motor activity involving the right arm, which typically presents as extension or, less frequently, flexion. Right arm involvement is often accompanied by a fencer-like posture, with the left arm remaining relaxed or flexed. In some instances, subtle bilateral arm flexion or rhythmic clonic jerks in the upper limbs are observed early in the episode. Head movements may include slight turning to the left or right, sometimes oscillating bilaterally, and are frequently associated with asymmetric limb involvement. This initial phase progresses in the majority of episodes to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, characterized by whole-body stiffening and rhythmic jerking. The spread to generalized tonic-clonic activity is described variably as progression, spread, or evolution, but consistently reflects secondary generalization. Rhythmic clonic jerks are commonly noted throughout the clonic phase. In one instance, the seizure activity remains limited to rhythmic clonic jerks in the upper limbs without significant generalization. Unresponsiveness is present during the course of the event, particularly toward the end. The overall semiology is highly stereotyped across episodes, with consistent features of staring, head version (predominantly rightward), unilateral right arm motor signs (extension more common than flexion), and subsequent evolution to bilateral tonic-clonic convulsions with rhythmic jerking.

In contrast, other segments show the patient seated calmly, engaged in activities such as reading or holding a snack, with no observable seizure activity, including absence of staring, automatisms, head deviation, or limb movements. One segment notes a leftward head version with subtle right arm flexion progressing to bilateral clonic jerking. Several reports consistently describe progression from a blank stare and subtle rightward head version with right arm involvement to bilateral tonic-clonic activity, while isolated instances lack significant motor features or generalization.
